data.title: White House preparing order to allow stockpiling metals from Pacific bottom: report
data.description:
The White House is preparing an executive order that will allow for stockpiling of metals found on the seabed of the Pacific Ocean, the Financial Times reported Saturday, citing people familiar with the matter. 

According to the report, the order would be a push to challenge the dominance by China of battery minerals and rare earth supply chains, the people reportedly said. 

The U.S. has been seeking to become more self-sufficient in accessing the minerals and announced moves to boost domestic output. 

The stockpile under consideration is part of a wider drive to fast-track applications for deep-sea mining and to establish onshore processing of nodules, the people familiar with the plans were quoted as saying. 

A stockpile would “create large quantities ready and available on US territory to be used in the future” if there is a dispute with China that would hinder metals and rare earths imports, another person familiar with the matter was quoted as saying. 

The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment by Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Insider trades: Nike, Kroger, GameStop among notable names this week
data.description:
Jotted below is a weekly record of insider trading, highlighting significant buy and sell transactions by investors, directors, and executives. The following transactions took place between April 7, 2025, and April 11, 2025.

  • Nike (NYSE:NKE) Director Robert Swan purchased 8,600 shares in the firm for $58.46 apiece for a total value of $502,756. Following the transaction, Swan currently owns 33,563 shares in the firm.
  • Ryan Cohen, CEO and chairman of GameStop (NYSE:GME) bought 500,00 shares of the firm for $21.55 apiece, amounting to a total of $10.7M. He now holds about 37M shares of the video game retailer.
  • Tilman Fertitta increased his stake in Wynn Resorts (NASDAQ:WYNN) after buying a total of 400,000 shares in the company at prices ranging from $67.62 to $70.39 per share, resulting in a cumulative investment of about $27.8M. Fertitta now holds 13M shares in the company.
  • Phillip Frost, CEO and chairman of OPKO Health (NASDAQ:OPK) revealed in a filing that he acquired 125,000 shares for $1.36 per share totaling $169,712. He now holds around 247M shares of the firm.
  • Asana’s (NYSE:ASAN) CEO and Chairman Dustin Moskovitz added to his shareholding in the firm by purchasing a total of 450,000 shares at prices ranging from $14.07 to $15.64 per share, for a total sum of about $6.7M. He now owns 55M shares in the company.
  • Christine Wheatley, executive vice president at Kroger (NYSE:KR) sold 30,000 shares of the company for $69.44 per share for a total worth of about $2M. She holds 127,737 shares in the company following the sale.
  • Dollar General’s (NYSE:DG) Chief Merchandising Officer Emily Taylor offloaded 809 shares of the company for $94.72, totaling $76,627. Taylor now holds 48,532 shares in the company.
  • Leon Black let go of 607,725 shares of Apollo Global Management (NYSE:APO) for $111.39 apiece for a total value of around $67.7M. He holds 33.9M shares of the firm following the sale.

data.title: Binance seeking less oversight while in talks with Trump crypto venture: report
data.description:
Binance executives met with officials with the Treasury Department last month to discuss loosening federal oversight of the cryptocurrency exchange operator, while it has been evaluating a possible deal with a crypto venture belonging to President Donald Trump’s family, The Wall Street Journal reported late Friday, citing people familiar with the talks. 

According to the report, some of the people said the Binance executives requested that the Treasury Department remove a government monitor that oversees the company’s compliance with anti-money-laundering laws. In 2023, Binance pleaded guilty to breaking those laws and agreed to pay $4.3B in fines. 

Other people reportedly said that Binance and the crypto venture backed by Trump’s family, World Liberty Financial, have been in talks about listing a new cryptocurrency pegged to the dollar. 

The report said the talking between Binance and Treasury officials after the company began talks with Trump family representatives. 

Binance, the White House, the Treasury Department, and World Liberty Financial did not immediately respond to requests for comment by Seeking Alpha.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Robinhood, Interactive Brokers climb, Rocket, UWMC dive in volatile week for financials
data.description:
Financial stocks (XLF) finished this week with a strong 5.6% surged as tariff developments continued to drive volatile price action, slightly trailing the S&P 500's 5.7% jump.

In a week also marked big banks kicking off  Q1 earnings season, Robinhood Markets (HOOD) rose the most of any financial stock, soaring 27%, even after Morgan Stanley downgraded the stock and crypto trading app to Equal-Weight;

From there, Interactive Brokers (IBKR), which during the week ended April 11 added more cryptocurrencies to its trading platform, gapped up 17%;

Ares Management (ARES) also gained 17% after Citizens upgraded the alternative asset manager to Market Perform on the back of its recent slump;

Argentine lender Banco Macro S.A. (BMA) accelerated 16%; and

Rounding out the five biggest winners, private equity giant Apollo Global Management (APO) shot up 16% in the same week it committed up to $400M for a new joint venture pact with commercial solar company Summit Ridge.

On the negative side, Rocket Companies (RKT) dropped 23%, despite Barclays upgrading the stock to Equalweight on the potential upside from its pending acquisition of Mr. Cooper (COOP);

UWM Holdings (UWMC) tanked 21%;

Mr. Cooper (COOP) shed 18%;

Fellow mortgage servicer PennyMac Financial Services (PFSI), which during the week KBW upgraded, retreated 10%; and

Regional lender Atlantic Union Bankshares (AUB) slid 6.5%.

data.title: Trump exempts smartphones, computers, chips from reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
Smartphones, computers and other electronics will be exempted from President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariffs, the U.S. Customs and Border Protection said on Friday. 

The exclusions, applicable to smartphones, laptops, computers, semiconductors, memory chips, flat panel TV displays, hard drives, provide some relief from Trump's 125% tariffs on China and 10% baseline tariffs on nearly every other country.

The guidance should provide relief to smartphone manufacturers like Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) and Samsung and chip major Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM). 

Apple (AAPL) reportedly airlifted 600 tons of iPhones to, or as many as 1.5 million, from India to the U.S., in an effort to beat the newly announced tariffs.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Apple, Eli Lilly and Verizon stocks among top picks
data.description:
The S&P500 (SP500) closed in the green on Friday, after the week saw volatility in markets due to fear that the escalation in the tariff war could fuel a global trade war and weigh on economic growth.

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) gained over 5%, while Dow (DJI) rose over 4%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Apple gets Jefferies upgrade after recent stock tumble

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) was upgraded to Hold from Underperform by Jefferies due to its recent stock price tumble amid risks associated with tariffs.

"Our base case remains that AAPL would be exempted from US tariffs, given its commitment to invest US$500bn in the US over the next four years, and our belief that it would make additional manufacturing investment commitment in the US (to make iPhone, for example)," said Jefferies analyst Edison Lee.

However, the brokerage lowered PT to $167.88 from $202.33 and reduced its iPhone shipment estimates for the next three fiscal years, saying rising risk of global recession could further impact already-weak iPhone demand.

Following comments from White House that Apple could build an iPhone in the U.S., BofA analysts said a U.S. built iPhone could cost 90% more.

Eli Lilly gets bullish rating from Goldman Sachs on attractive entry point

Goldman Sachs upgraded Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) to Buy from Neutral, arguing that at the current trading levels there is a “compelling entry point into the sector’s premier topline grower.”

Commenting on LLY’s leadership in the anti-obesity drug industry, where its rival Novo Nordisk (NVO) also operates, analyst Asad Haider said that the company is expected to maintain its pole position in a market projected to triple in size from ~$28B currently to ~$95B by 2030.

However, Goldman Sachs revised down its price target on LLY to $888 from $892.

Verizon is Evercore's top wireless pick

Evercore upgraded Verizon (NYSE:VZ) to Outperform from In line and named the stock its top pick under the broader wireless sector.

Evercore is optimistic about Verizon’s broadband strategy across fiber and fixed wireless and added that there’s a meaningful perception gap between telecom’s fiber efforts and its peers, particularly AT&T. The brokerage hiked PT on the stock by $4 to $48.

Citi bullish on Diamondback Energy, downgrades California Resources, Vital Energy

Citi upgraded Diamondback Energy (NASDAQ:FANG) to Buy from Neutral, with a $180 price target, raised from $157, saying the stock's recent selloff presents an opportunity to buy a high-quality E&P "discounting ~$58/bbl WTI into perpetuity."

Citi's Scott Gruber says Diamondback remains a top-tier E&P name as reflected in a breakeven oil price at just ~$36/bbl, the lowest among the bank's coverage, enabling free cash generation even when crude prices are depressed.

Meanwhile, the brokerage downgraded California Resources (NYSE:CRC) and Vital Energy (NYSE:VTLE) to Neutral from Buy with respective $36 and $17 PTs, expecting higher cost, small-cap stocks likely will take longer to recover from current conditions given the macro uncertainty.

Bath & Body Works favorite among teens, Piper upgrades

Piper Sandler upgraded Bath & Body Works (NYSE:BBWI) to Overweight from Neutral, as it continued to hold the top spot as the favorite fragrance for teen females, beating out luxury rivals like Dior, Chanel, and Marc Jacobs.

With less than 10% of its merchandise sourced from China—and the majority produced in the U.S.—BBWI faces minimal exposure to tariff risk, according to Piper Sandler’s Korinne Wolfmeyer, adding that the firm sees sufficient upside to raise its revenue and EPS estimates for FY25 and FY26 by ~0.4% to 0.5%.

GM, Ford, Lear, Visteon in focus after tariff announcement

UBS downgraded General Motors (NYSE:GM) to Neutral from Buy and lowered its earnings estimates for the stock, accounting for the impact of tariffs on the company’s cost structure along with the impact to auto demand.

Goldman Sachs also downgraded Ford Motor (NYSE:F) to Neutral from Buy, saying its earlier bullish stance, initiated in September 2024, was overly optimistic. The bank also downgraded auto suppliers Lear (NYSE:LEA) and Visteon (NASDAQ:VC) to Neutral from Buy, pointing to the sector's inability to fully offset lower industry volumes. 

GS cited growing concerns over global competition, weakening consumer demand and rising costs from tariffs behind the downgrades.

Piper Sandler upgraded Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) to Overweight from Neutral as the bank moves to offense from defense and due to its quicker pace of regulatory resolution. "WFC is having its most visible success in years on the regulatory front, and we expect further progress ultimately to relieve the company of what has been a significant company-specific headwind," the analyst said.

Benchmark reiterated its bullish view on Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and added the electric vehicle stock to its Best Ideas list after watching shares fall sharply this year. Analyst Mickey Legg said the recent sales declines are significant, but thinks the share price decline is overblown considering the scope of opportunities around the corner.

Morgan Stanley reiterated its Overweight rating for Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) and listed it as its top pick under media and entertainment stocks, replacing Disney (DIS). MS analysts expect the streaming giant to demonstrate relative resilience in a weaker global macroeconomic situation.

Citi Research upgraded Dollar Tree (NASDAQ:DLTR) to Buy from Neutral, with analyst Paul Lejuez saying that while the company was previously trying to manage China tariffs within its current pricing architecture, this higher tariff regime gives them further cover to expand price points from $1.25 to $1.50 - $1.75.

data.title: Earnings Scorecard: 8 out of 10 companies beat EPS estimates with big wins from banks
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages on Friday experienced dizzying swings as market participants digested further tariff escalation, a host of quarterly earnings from major banks and more inflation data. 

Markets roared earlier this week, reaching historic highs after U.S. President Donald Trump announced a pause on reciprocal tariffs against most countries except China. 

However, the market’s euphoria disappeared soon as it came to grips with the fact that it was just a short-term relief and that longer-term recession risks were still very much real.

BofA analysts believe that investors should short the S&P 500 (SP500) until the Federal Reserve “cuts hard” to break the liquidation cycle, and until the U.S. and China pause their trade war and reverse the global recession. 

Michael Hartnett, chief investment strategist at BofA, said the era of the U.S. “exceptionalism” is ending, and the era of a “U.S. repudiation” is starting. “It is the end of the ‘U.S. buys rest of the world goods,’ and ‘the rest of the world buys U.S. Treasuries.’” 

Earnings Roundup: 

Amidst the tariff frenzy, a primarily green earnings scorecard brought much needed cheer to the markets. Out of the ten companies that reported earnings this week, eight of them beat EPS expectations, while all of them topped revenue estimates. 

Sector-wise performance:

Consumer: Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA) beat both second quarter non-GAAP EPS and revenue estimates. The company reported EPS of $0.63, on a revenue of $38.6B. However, the company withdrew its fiscal 2025 guidance given the pending transaction, pursuant to which WBA will be acquired by entities affiliated with Sycamore Partners. 

Constellation Brands (STZ), the parent company of Corona beer, earned a profit of $2.63 per share, up from $2.30 a year ago and 36 cents better than expected. This was achieved on a small improvement in sales to $2.164B, topping estimates of $2.12B. 

Looking ahead to FY26, Constellation Brands expects earnings to be between $12.60 - $12.90 versus the consensus estimate of $13.94 per share. 

Financial: Wells Fargo (WFC) delivered Q1 earnings that topped Wall Street estimates, helped by strength in its Corporate and Investment Banking and Wealth and Investment Management businesses.

The bank reaffirmed its 2025 net interest income guidance, expecting an increase of ~1%-3% Y/Y. Noninterest expense is expected to be ~$54.2B, also unchanged from prior guidance.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) reaffirmed 2025 guidance for net interest income, excluding markets, and turned in stronger-than-expected Q1 2025 earnings, bolstered by robust gains in investment banking and markets revenue.

The company reaffirmed its 2025 guidance for net interest income, excluding markets, at ~$90B. 

BlackRock (BLK) also turned in better-than-expected Q1 earnings, as the company delivered broad-based annualized organic base fee growth, driven by private markets, ETFs and systematic active strategies. 

The company’s Q1 adjusted EPS of $11.30 topped the average analyst estimate of $10.21 and revenue of $5.28B, missed the $5.31B consensus. 

Industrials: Delta Air Lines (DAL) topped earnings expectations with its Q1 earnings, but pulled its full-year guidance due to the macroeconomic backdrop. 

CEO Ed Bastian highlighted that with broad economic uncertainty around global trade, growth has largely stalled. 

Earnings next week:

The next week will see a bunch of companies report earnings, such as banking giant Goldman Sachs (GS) on Monday, and Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and United Airlines (UAL) on Tuesday. 

Wednesday will see quarterly reports from Abbott (ABT), CSX Corporation (CSX), and US Bancorp (USB), while Thursday will include UnitedHealth Group (UNH), State Street (STT), and Netflix (NFLX).

data.title: NXP, ON, Mobileye, Allegro Microsystems on watch if tariffs hurt auto demand: Loop Capital
data.description:
NXP Semiconductors (NASDAQ:NXPI), ON Semiconductor (NASDAQ:ON), Mobileye (NASDAQ:MBLY) and Allegro Microsystems (NASDAQ:ALGM) could be impacted if tariffs hurt automotive demand, Loop Capital said.

“Now that we're in a tariff war with China, and considering automotive import tariffs (at 25%) remain in place, we think automotive chip executives will incorporate a high degree of cautiousness into the remaining CY25 outlooks,” analyst Gary Mobley wrote in a note to investors. “It is our view that the inflationary impact of tariffs on new vehicles prices should cause consumers to postpone purchases.”

Mobley, who has Buy ratings on all four companies, said prior to the introduction of tariffs, the automotive part of the semiconductor market looked “encouraging.” Bookings were modestly improving, inventories were declining and there seemed to be a pathway towards “modest” sequential revenue improvements throughout the rest of 2025. But now, all of that is out the window, with massive uncertainty happening.

Looking at the near-term, Mobley said these and other automotive-levered chip names could see upside to top-line estimates, but that may just be due to pull-forward as supply chains prepared for the coming of tariffs.

In the longer-term, the sector does have several tailwinds going for it, including: the electrification of the powertrain; advanced driverless assistance systems; connectivity and software defined vehicles. But all of that has been overshadowed by tariffs, which could raise the price of a vehicle by “several thousand” dollars, and in some cases, potentially $20,000 or more.

The global automotive market has been rattled by the tariffs, notably the U.S. market, which accounts for roughly 16% to 17% of all light vehicle sales. And with China ratcheting up its levies on imports coming from the U.S. to match those from the U.S., ultimately that could result in 1.8M fewer vehicles being sold in the U.S. and Canada, Mobley said.

data.title: Beam Global targets positive cash flow in 2025 with expanded product lineup and geographic reach
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Beam Global (BEEM) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Desmond Wheatley noted that Beam Global’s 2024 revenue doubled compared to previous years, reaching $49.3 million, and emphasized the company's five-year revenue CAGR of 68%. He attributed this to investments in sales resources and engineering improvements that reduced costs.
  • The company achieved gross margins of 15%, with adjusted gross margins net of non-cash items at 21%. Wheatley highlighted that cost-saving measures, including material discounts and operational efficiencies, contributed to this improvement.
  • Wheatley announced the launch of new products like BeamSpot, BeamBike, BeamWell, and BeamPatrol, designed to diversify revenue streams. He emphasized that these new offerings cater to distinct market needs such as disaster zones, urban mobility, and law enforcement.
  • CFO Lisa Potok stated that cash on hand was $4.6 million at the end of 2024, compared to $10.4 million in 2023, with cash use focused on acquisitions and operational needs.
  • Wheatley discussed the strategic importance of Beam’s geographic expansion, particularly in Europe, the Middle East, and Africa, as a response to challenges in the U.S. market.

Outlook

  • Management projected that the company is on a trajectory to achieve positive cash flow in 2025, even with flat revenue compared to 2024. Wheatley indicated that gross profit trends and operational efficiencies will support this goal.
  • Wheatley mentioned that the first quarter of 2025 is expected to reflect similar challenges as Q4 2024, but the company anticipates growth in later quarters driven by its expanded product lineup and international sales focus.

Financial Results

  • Beam Global reported a loss from operations of $11.7 million for 2024, down from $16.3 million in 2023. Adjusted non-GAAP loss from operations was $8.9 million in 2024 compared to $11.8 million in 2023.
  • Gross profit for 2024 was $10.4 million, with significant contributions from cost reductions and operational efficiencies.
  • Operating expenses increased to $19 million in 2024, primarily due to new hires, marketing expenses, and acquisition-related costs.
  • Recurring revenue initiatives, such as sponsorship models, have begun contributing to the company’s income, with initial success at Belgrade International Airport.

Q&A

  • Tate Sullivan, Maxim Group: Asked about new product timelines and sales expectations. Wheatley responded that products like BeamBike and BeamWell have already seen initial sales, with BeamWell units en route to the Middle East.
  • Sullivan also inquired about recurring revenue sources. Wheatley stated that sponsorship models and data services from smart city products are key areas for future recurring revenue growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on the potential of new products and international expansion while acknowledging challenges in the U.S. federal market.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing resilience and strategic diversification to mitigate risks. Wheatley reiterated, “We are on track to achieve positive cash flow this year.”

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue for Q4 2024 declined compared to Q3, reflecting reduced federal sales and order timing issues. However, enterprise sales increased, making up 64% of Q4 orders versus 47.9% in Q3.
  • Gross margin improved year-over-year, but quarterly variability remained due to lower volumes impacting overhead absorption.
  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to a more assertive focus on international and product diversification in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Wheatley identified challenges from U.S. federal policy changes, particularly reduced government incentives for electric vehicles and infrastructure.
  • Management highlighted tariffs and rising material costs as potential risks but noted the mitigating impact of European operations, which allow for cost-efficient production outside the U.S.
  • Analysts raised concerns about delayed UL certifications, which have impacted order fulfillment timelines.

Final Takeaway

Beam Global’s Q4 2024 earnings call showcased the company’s focus on mitigating U.S. market challenges through international expansion and a diversified product portfolio. With innovative offerings like BeamBike and BeamWell, alongside strategic cost-saving measures, the company is positioned to achieve positive cash flow in 2025. Management’s emphasis on recurring revenue and geographic reach underscores its resilience amid federal policy uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold’s latest record-breaking run spurred by buying rush in daily auctions
data.description:
This week's surge in gold prices to fresh records was fueled by a rush of buying activity in daily auctions, a sign that major players in the market are still adding to their holdings even at all-time high prices, Bloomberg reported Friday.

Bids for more than 400K oz of gold worth $1.3B were registered in the opening round of the afternoon auction on Thursday, the largest such volume since September 2019, according to a Bloomberg analysis of data from ICE Benchmark Administration, which runs the auctions.

Traders and analysts usually relate major buying or selling activity in the auctions to central banks, which have been a major driver in gold prices rising above $3,000/oz, but other groups of market participants also have been big buyers recently, with ETFs adding 5.8M oz of gold YTD, Bloomberg said.

Gold futures settled above $3,200/oz for the first time on Friday, as a weakening dollar and escalating U.S.-China trade war ramped up recession fears, sending investors flocking to the traditional safety of the metal.

"Gold is clearly seen as the favored safe-haven asset in a world upended by Trump's trade war," WisdomTree commodities strategist Nitesh Shah said. "The U.S. dollar has depreciated, and U.S. Treasuries are selling off hard, as faith in the U.S. as a reliable trading partner has diminished."

Elsewhere, a softer than expected Consumer Price Index report has raised hopes the Federal Reserve will ease monetary policy, which would be another boon for bullion.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery closed +7% this week at a new record-high settlement of $3,222.20/oz, a fourth consecutive daily gain and the largest one-week percentage gain since the week ending March 27, 2020, while front-month April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished +9.3% to $31.824/oz for the week, its best weekly percentage gain since the week ending September 13, 2024; on Friday, gold and silver rose 2.1% and 3.7%, respectively.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

Current gold prices look well-supported given market turmoil caused by U.S. tariff policy, Commerzbank analysts said in raising their year-end price forecast for spot gold to $3,000/oz from $2,850/oz previously, and UBS analysts now expect gold prices will hit $3,500/oz this year, expecting gold's rally will "extend into next year and for prices to stabilize at higher levels further out."

Earlier this week, Deutsche Bank raised its average gold price forecasts for 2025 and 2026 to $3,139/oz and $3,700/oz, respectively.

Gold ETFs posted strong inflows of 92 metric tons in March, bringing Q1 inflows to a three-year high of 226 tons, with the biggest inflows coming from U.S.-listed ETFs, although European and Asian ETFs also showed inflows.
